summary:
The Egyptian Late Period (664–332 BC) was the final phase of ancient Egypt as an independent civilization — a complex era that saw native Egyptian dynasties (the 26th Saite and 30th dynasties) alternating with Persian occupation (27th and 31st dynasties) before Alexander the Great's conquest in 332 BC ended Egyptian independence forever. The period was characterized by cultural renaissance (deliberate revival of classical Egyptian artistic and literary styles) alongside political vulnerability.

The Late Period's defining dynamic was the challenge of maintaining Egyptian independence against the great powers of the Near East — Assyria, Persia, and eventually Macedonia. The Saite dynasty (664–525 BC) achieved a remarkable recovery, hiring Greek mercenaries, reopening trade with the Aegean, and building impressive monuments. The Persian conquest under Cambyses (525 BC) inaugurated the first Persian period (27th dynasty), followed by a period of native revolt and the Libyan and Nectanebo dynasties, before Persia reconquered Egypt in 343 BC. The Persians were expelled by Alexander just eleven years later. The Late Period's cultural output included significant religious and funerary literature (the Book of the Dead reached its definitive form), massive temple construction at Luxor, Karnak, and Edfu, and the first systematic mummification techniques.

read_more_links.title: Book of the Dead Late Period
